title: From Lab Joke to 'Smart Underwear': Sensor Tracks Daily Farts to Establish a Normal Baseline
summary: How many times do you fart a day? Until now, science had no answer. That changed when Brantley Hall, a microbiologist at the University of Maryland, got frustrated with a failed lab experiment. "We took the sensor out of the chamber, and we were like, 'Screw it. We're going to try to measure a fart,'" he recalls. Hall stuck the device down his pants and let rip — the signal was enormous. That moment of levity led to the development of "smart underwear": a quarter-sized sensor that snaps into regular undies to track hydrogen, a key component of flatulence. In a study published in Biosensors and Bioelectronics: X, healthy college-age volunteers wore the device. The results? An average of 32 farts per day, but with staggering variation — from just 4 to as many as 59. High-fiber gumdrops increased frequency in nearly all participants. Hall was stunned by the lack of medical knowledge on the topic. "If you go to the doctor, they don't know the normal number of farts," he says. "If you tell them, 'I'm farting 50 times a day,' they don't have a baseline to compare that to." The team has now launched the Human Flatus Atlas, a larger study where volunteers wear the sensor around the clock (except during showers) and log meals via an app. The sensor is unobtrusive — participants have run 5Ks and played rugby — but biking is off-limits due to seat pressure. Early findings suggest three farting phenotypes: "zen digesters" who rarely toot even on high-fiber diets, "hydrogen hyperproducers" at the opposite extreme, and "normal people" in between. The project aims to establish a healthy range, potentially aiding in diagnosing gut disorders. With over 3,500 people expressing interest, the initial 800 sensors were snapped up in days. Hall hopes the Atlas will fill a bizarre gap in human physiology. "[In] 2026, we don't know if people are farting at night or not," he laughs. "Isn't that funny?"
why_it_matters: This project fills a surprisingly empty chapter of human biology. A reliable flatulence baseline could eventually help doctors spot digestive disorders early, while normalizing a bodily function that everyone experiences—but almost no one studies seriously. For teens, understanding that even science can have a sense of humor about the human body helps break down stigma around health data.

keywords.explanation: The medical term for the passing of intestinal gas from the body, commonly called farting.
A gas that is one of the main components produced by bacteria in the gut and released during flatulence.
A standard or normal starting point that scientists use to compare measurements and see what is unusual.
Observable traits or patterns—here, describing different types of flatulence behavior among people.
A scientist who studies microscopic organisms, including the bacteria living in the human gut.
The name of a large ongoing study that aims to map and understand the healthy range of human flatulence using wearable sensors.

What term does the article use for people who rarely pass gas even on a high-fiber diet?

background_read: Researchers have long known that gut bacteria break down food and produce gases like hydrogen, carbon dioxide, and sometimes methane, but until now, no large-scale study had attempted to measure what 'normal' looks like in daily life outside a laboratory.

Brantley Hall is a microbiologist at the University of Maryland, an institution known for its interdisciplinary research linking microbiology and bioengineering. His team's work was published in Biosensors and Bioelectronics: X, a specialist journal focused on new sensor technologies for biological applications.

Flatulence is a natural part of digestion, yet it remains a social taboo; medical textbooks offer almost no guidance on what constitutes a healthy frequency or the extreme variations that exist among people. The Human Flatus Atlas aims to build the first large, real-world dataset by distributing over 800 quarter-sized sensors to volunteers who log meals and activities.

The giggle-inducing origin story—that Hall stuck a failed lab sensor into his pants as a joke—reveals how accidental discoveries can lead to serious science, filling a genuinely blank spot in human physiology.
Article_Structure: LEAD: How many farts a day is normal? Science had no answer—until Brantley Hall's failed lab experiment turned into a wearable-sensor project.
└─ hook: 'We took the sensor out of the chamber, and we were like, "Screw it. We're going to try to measure a fart."'
SENSOR DEVELOPMENT: A quarter-sized, snap-in device that tracks hydrogen; it evolved from a joking attempt into serious research.
STUDY FINDINGS: Average 32 per day, but huge variation (4–59); high-fiber gumdrops increased frequency; three farting phenotypes identified.
THE BIG GAP: Doctors lack a baseline to interpret patient complaints—this is what the Human Flatus Atlas is designed to provide.
REAL-WORLD USABILITY: Sensor works around the clock (except showers); participants have run 5Ks and played rugby, but biking is off-limits.
OUTLOOK: Over 3,500 people expressed interest, 800 sensors quickly distributed; the project may one day help diagnose gut disorders.

perspectives.description: Having a scientifically validated range of normal flatulence could become a cheap, non-invasive screening tool for gut health problems like irritable bowel syndrome, lactose intolerance, or small intestinal bacterial overgrowth—conditions that often go undiagnosed because patients are too embarrassed to mention the symptom or doctors dismiss it.

A study of healthy college-age volunteers is too narrow to represent all ages, diets, and health conditions; the 'normal' range it establishes may not apply widely. Additionally, wearable sensors that track intimate bodily functions raise privacy concerns if data ends up in the wrong hands, such as insurance companies or employers.

The project highlights a broader pattern in medical research: everyday bodily functions like flatulence, burping, or hiccups are often treated as too trivial to investigate, leaving large gaps in knowledge. The huge variation seen here—4 to 59 daily emissions—shows how little we know about even basic human physiology and the influence of diet and microbiome.

If the Human Flatus Atlas matures, future versions of the sensor might pair with smartphone apps to alert users when their digestive gas patterns deviate from their personal baseline, flagging potential trouble before other symptoms appear. This could mark a shift toward real-time, non-invasive monitoring of gut health in everyday life.
